coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Au@nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots@Pt core-shell structure nanoparticles for enhanced methanol electrooxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Sen
Wang, Yan
Wang, Li'na
Guo, Tong
Yuan, Xiaorong
Zhang, Dongxia
Xue, Zhonghua
Zhou, Xibin
Lu, Xiaoquan
coredata.dc:description: Here, a unique triple-layered core-shell structure (Au@N-CQDs@Pt) with an Au metal core, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots interlayer, and metal Pt outside shell was obtained by chemical reduction of HAuCl4 and H2PtCl6 in the presence of N-CQDs. The remarkable advantage of the method reported here is no any stabilizer or surfactant agent involved. The results of transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) reveal that the as-prepared nanocomposites exhibit the nanostructured dendritic Pt and the quantity of Pt shell can be easily changed through controlling the synthesis time. Specifically, Au@N-CQDs@Pt displays excellent catalytic activity toward methanol oxidation reaction in alkaline condition than Au-Pt without N-CQDs, which indicate the useful application of CQDs in designing new catalysts to be used in direct methanol fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Au-Pt bimetallic nanoparticles
Core-shell nanostructure
Methanol electrooxidation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CQDs; inset: corresponding size distribution. (B) TEM image of Au@N-CQDs; inset: corresponding size distribution. (C) HRTEM images of several Au@N-CQDs. (D) HRTEM images of single Au@N-CQDs.
(A) TEM image of Au@N-CQDs@Pt; inset: corresponding size distribution. (B) TEM images of single Au@N-CQDs@Pt; inset: HRTEM images of Au@N-CQDs@Pt.
(a) HAADF-STEM image of Au@N-CQDs@Pt. (b) Au element mapping of single Au@N-CQDs@Pt. (c) Pt element mapping of single Au@N-CQDs@Pt. (d) Composite of Au and Pt elements mappings.
(a) Wide-angle XRD patterns of CQDs, (b) Au@N-CQDs, and (c) Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45.
XPS spectra of Pt 4f (A) and Au 4f (B) of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles with the reduction of AA for 45 min.
TEM image of Au@N-CQDs@Pt with different reaction time after AA addition: (A–B) 10 min, (C–D) 45 min and (E–F) 90 min.
UV–visible absorption spectra of Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles recorded at different reaction times: (a) 0 min, (b) 10 min, (c) 30 min, (d) 45 min, (e) 60 min, (f) 90 min.
Photographs of catalysts suspensions taken at different times: (a) 0 min, (b) 10 min, (c) 30 min, (d) 45 min, (e) 60 min, (f) 90 min.

(A) CV curves of Au@N-CQDs@Pt-10, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-30, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45 and Au@N-CQDs@Pt-60 in 1 M NaOH solution containing 0.5 M methanol with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (B) CV curves of Au@N-CQDs@Pt-10, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-30, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45 and Au@N-CQDs@Pt-60 in 0.5 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (C) CA curves of Au@N-CQDs@Pt-10, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-30, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45 and Au@N-CQDs@Pt-60 in 1 M NaOH solution containing 0.5 M methanol.

CV of Au@N-CQDs@Pt, Au-Pt and Pt/C catalysts modified electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 (A), 1 M NaOH solution containing 0.5 M methanol (B). Chronoamperometric curves of Au@N-CQDs@Pt, Au-Pt and Pt/C catalysts modified electrodes in 1 M NaOH solution containing 0.5 M methanol (C).
The preparation of Au@N-CQDs@Pt process and it application for methanol electrooxidation.
Binding energies of Pt 4f with their relative intensities, and the atomic compositions of different Pt species.
Comparison of as-obtained catalysts for methanol electrooxidation.
Summary results of the electrooxidation of methanol by the Au@N-CQDs@Pt, Au-Pt nanocomposites, Pt/N-CQDs and the commercial Pt/C catalyst.

row.entry: 3000 s in i-t curve (mA mg−1 Pt)

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Au@nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots@Pt core-shell structure nanoparticles for enhanced methanol electrooxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
College of Geography and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a unique triple-layered core-shell structure (Au@N-CQDs@Pt) with an Au metal core, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots interlayer, and metal Pt outside shell was obtained by chemical reduction of HAuCl4 and H2PtCl6 in the presence of N-CQDs. The remarkable advantage of the method reported here is no any stabilizer or surfactant agent involved. The results of transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) reveal that the as-prepared nanocomposites exhibit the nanostructured dendritic Pt and the quantity of Pt shell can be easily changed through controlling the synthesis time. Specifically, Au@N-CQDs@Pt displays excellent catalytic activity toward methanol oxidation reaction in alkaline condition than Au-Pt without N-CQDs, which indicate the useful application of CQDs in designing new catalysts to be used in direct methanol fuel cells.

Schematic of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt preparation process and it application for methanol electrooxidation.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional fuels have already come a long way, resulting in two dramatic problems: environment pollution and energy crisis. Advanced sustainable energy conversion device represent a promising technology for development of clean energy to change the world's energy future. In the past decades, scientists and engineers have long believed fuel cells can be next-generation power generator for application in electronic products and electric cars [1–3]. In the field of fuel cells, direct methanol fuel cells (DMCFs) have been creeping into the mainstream, because of their outstanding advantages of higher energy density, safe structure, and low pollution as well as the easy transporting of methanol as a liquid fuel [4,5]. Nevertheless, wide-range commercial viability of fuel cells is still the difficult challenges because of high production cost and sluggish methanol oxidation kinetics. Along this direction, the development of advanced and efficient anode catalysts have been considered as a one of the crucial directions [6]. In particular, the mainstream belief among scientists is that Pt is the first choice catalyst for the methanol oxidation reactions (MOR) in fuel cells. However, massive application of Pt are pretty hard because of high price, and poison effect of intermediate species [7,8]. Therefore, new catalysts with improved stability and lower consumption of precious metal Pt are strongly needed.

Inspired by those simultaneous challenges, intensive research have been focused on the rational fabrication or modification of various Pt-based nanomaterials with suitable size and ideal compositions [9,10]. Up to now, a good chunk of electrocatalyst have been successfully synthesized in ways that combination Pt with abundant metals [11–15]. The promoted property of these materials has been attribute to some factors, for instance, the synergistic action of bimetallic materials, d band center move and lattice changing [16,17]. Compared with many Pt-based binary catalysts, incorporation of Au into the Pt system has been verified to possess excellent catalytic performance for methanol oxidation, hence hold great promise as perfect anode catalysts in DMFCs [18,19].

As an extra factor to transform the performances of electrocatalyst, morphology of the catalyst play an essential role in changing the specific areas and facets [20,21]. Last few years, a variety of electrocatalysts with desirable nanostructures have been widely reported, like nanospheres [22], nanorods [23], nanoflowers [24], nanodendrites [25], core-shell [26] and so on. Among these structures, core-shell structure are flourishing than ever before, because these nanoparticles have been applied in the high-tech field between materials science and many other scientific fields, such as optics, pharmacy, biomedicine and electronics [27]. On the other hand, core-shell nanoparticles can be modified with a lot of functional properties and their unique structure is helpful to generate a large number of surface active sites and finally resulting in the great increase of electrocatalytic properties as well as decreasing the dosage of precious metal.

In order to further strengthen the electroactivity of Pt-based catalysts and reducing the consumption of nobel metal, it is necessary to load metal nanoparticles on the supporting materials which have several special properties, for example, large surface area, excellent stability and high electrical conductivity. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new carbonaceous material, have been used in a diverse range of fields owing to a number of advantages, such as excellent conductivity, good hydrophilicity, outstanding optical properties, low cytotoxicity and so on [28–32]. Besides, CQDs also introduced as oxidizer or reductant since the hydroxyl and carboxy segments on its surface can be electron acceptors and electron donors during different reaction processes [33–35]. The functional group of CQDs also can make them to be the nucleation centers for the growth of metallic nanoparticles [36,37]. Moreover, CQDs can activate water through a rapid route, thus speed up the transformation rate of intermediates CO to CO2 for relieve catalyst poisoning [38]. In recent years, small CQDs with superior properties were extensively employed as electrocatalyst supports for methanol oxidation. For example, Zhang et al. [39] reported a facial hydrothermal treatment to synthesize NCQDs-MWCNT support, which acts as an improved catalyst support of Pt-based anode catalyst for direct methanol fuel full cells. The results of electrochemical measurements revel that Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst exhibits 1.3 times higher activity for methanol electrooxidation than that of Pt/MWCNT, which means NCQDs play a critical role in electrocatalytic performance for methanol electrooxidation.

The structure of carbon materials can be changed through heteroatoms doping, leading to the improved intrinsic properties, including electronic structures, electron conductivity and chemical state [40,41]. Among them, nitrogen-doped carbon materials can induce a negative charge with respect to the delocalized sp2-hybridized carbon framework, resulting in enhanced electron transfer [42]. In addition, N species on the carbon support can lead to high dispersion of fine Pt nanoparticles with the synergistic interaction of Pt and support, thus can improve the catalytic activity and stability toward methanol oxidation reaction [43]. Some studies also have shown that N-CQDs with abundant structural defects can promote charges transfer from catalysts to reactants as well as to improve the dispersal of Pt catalysts. Such as, Zhang et al. [44] obtained N-CQDs by treating rice husk-based carbon, and then prepared Pt catalysts supported on N-CQDs (Pt/N-CQDs). The researchers found Pt/N-CQDs catalysts presented a high electrocatalytic activity and stability for methanol oxidation compared to commercial Pt/C catalysts (Pt/XC-72).

In view of these, if we can take advantages of both Au-Pt bimetallic core-shell structure and N-CQDs, preparation of Pt-based catalysts with enhanced electrocatalytic performance and durability will be realized. In this contribution, Au@N-CQDs@Pt core-shell structure nanoparticles were obtained through a facile two steps process. In brief, Au@N-CQDs was firstly prepared by reducing HAuCl4 with N-CQDs at 100 °C. Then ascorbic acid (AA) was served to reduce H2PtCl6 in the presence of Au@N-CQDs to obtain the final product at room temperature. Furthermore, the as-prepared nanocomposites showed a good electrocatalytic activities and stability in alkaline methanol as compared to commercial Pt/C catalysts. The fabrication procedure is illustrates in Scheme 1 .

Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) and Chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium citrate dehydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), ammonium bicarbonate (NH4HCO3) was obtained from Tianjin Kaixin Chemical Reagent Company. Pt/C (20%) was subscribed from shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Nafion solution (5% wt) was obtained from DUPONT. The reagents and chemicals employed in this paper were of analytical reagent grade. The water used in this work was doubly distilled water.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image measurements of the as-prepared materials were examined by FEI-Tecnai G2 TF20 electron microscope and the accelerating voltage set as 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured by a Rigaku D/max-2400 diffractometer (Cu Kα1 0.154056 nm radiation, 2θ angle range 5–90°). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was gained on an electron microscope (FEI-Tecnai G2 TF20) system equipped with an EDX detector. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by PHI-5702 (USA). Thermogravimetry analyses (TGA) were carried out using a STA 499 C TG-DSC thermogravimetric analyzer and the heating rate set as 10 °C min−1 under air condition from 20 to 800 °C. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) was recorded by a spectrometer named New Century T6 provided by Purkinje General Instrument Company (China). Raman spectra were obtained by inVia Rensishaw confocal spectroscopy (Britain) with 633 nm laser excitation. Fourier-transform infrared spectra were recorded on DIGILAB FTS-3000 FTIR spectrometer. All electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation subscribed from Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Company.

The synthesis of N-CQDs was carried out through the hydrothermal method according to a published paper [45]. In brief, Na3C6H5O7·2H2O (1.2 g) and NH4HCO3 (9 g) were dissolved into 60 mL water, which than placed in a Teflon equipped with autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was subject to hydrothermal reaction with the condition of 180 °C for 4 h in a drying oven (DHG-9030A, Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Limited Company). After that, the mixture was treating with a dialysis bag (8000–14000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for 72 h. Finally, the obtained N-CQDs were dried for 48 h at 60 °C.

Au@N-CQDs was synthesized by reducing HAuCl4 with N-CQDs according to the method [46]. Briefly, 2.4 mL HAuCl4 (2.5 mM) aqueous solution was put into 10 mL N-CQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) aqueous solution. After that, the mixture was incubated in oil bath with the reaction temperature of 100 °C for 80 min to develop a homogeneous purple suspension of Au@N-CQDs.

In order to obtain core-shell Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles, 2.4 mL H2PtCl6 (2.5 mM) was put into the Au@N-CQDs colloid solution, and then maintain stirring for 5 min. Ascorbic acid (AA, 180 μL, 0.1 mM), function as reductant, was put into the mixture at ambient temperature. The resulting solution was reacting for 45 min, as it turned black, the Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles were generated. To explore the electrocatalytic performance of Au@N-CQDs@Pt catalysts synthesized with different reaction times, the reaction was proceeding for 10 min, 30 min, 45min, 60 min and 90 min were named as Au@N-CQDs@Pt-10, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-30, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-60 and Au@N-CQDs@Pt-90.

For synthesis of Pt/N-CQDs, 2.4 mL H2PtCl6 and 10 mL N-CQDs mixed together under stirring. After homogeneous mixing, 2 mL of freshly prepared sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 4 mg mL−1) added to the mixed solution under stirring. The mixture was reacting for 3 h at room temperature for preparing Pt/N-CQDs.

All electrochemical performance was tested using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation equipped with a three-electrode system. A Pt wire electrode as auxiliary electrode, a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 3 mm) as working electrode and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) used as reference electrode. For the modification of working electrode, 2 mg prepared composite and 6 μL of Nafion (0.05% wt) dispersed in 2 mL water to generate an homogeneous ink, then 5 μL of the ink deposited on the surface of GCE. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) experiments were carried out in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution and 1 M NaOH+0.5 M methanol solution. The chronoamperometry (CA) experiments were also conducted to estimate the electrocatalytic endurance. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded at frequencies between 0.1 to 104 Hz in a solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4- and 0.1 M KCl. The solutions used in experiments were purged with N2 (99.99%) for 30 min to remove dissolved oxygen before use.

TEM image reveal that the N-CQDs are well dispersed with uniform size of 3.75 nm were obtained (Fig. 1 A), meanwhile, as can been seen from its UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S1A), there were two absorption features at 340 nm and 235 nm. The peak at 235 nm arises from the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic CC sp2 domains, and the other peak at 340 nm is caused by the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states [47,48]. The inset picture of N-CQDs displayed bright blue light under UV light with emission at 365 nm. The fluorescence (FL) excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs are shows in Fig. S1B. With the maximum FL emission peak centered at 438 nm when excited at 377 nm, like most other fluorescence N-CQDs, these as-synthesized N-CQDs show an excitation-dependent FL behavior (Fig. S1C).

Fig. 1B shows TEM images of the Au@N-CQDs nanoparticles, from which can be seen Au@N-CQDs formed sphere-like structure with the average size of 11 ± 2 nm. The high magnification images of several Au@N-CQDs (Fig. 1C) clearly illustrate that the cores are covered by contiguous substance of about 3 nm thickness which is approximate to the size of N-CQDs, revealing the formation of Au@N-CQDs nanoparticles. Moreover, the HRTEM image of single Au@N-CQDs (Fig. 2 D) evidenced clear lattice distances of 0.215 nm and 0.235 nm are consistent with the (100) crystallographic planes of graphitic carbon [39] and (111) planes of crystalline Au [32], respectively. Different from N-CQDs, Au@N-CQDs nanoparticles display a representative absorbance in 530 nm in UV–vis absorption spectrum, which is caused by the surface plasmon band of Au nanoparticles (Fig. S1D) [49]. And the quenching of fluorescence emissions of N-CQDs after the addition of HAuCl4 further indicates the formation of Au@N-CQDs nanoparticles (Fig. S1E).

The morphology and nanostructure of Au@N-CQDs@Pt is also investigated by TEM (Fig. 2A and B). Different from the smooth spherical Au@N-CQDs nanoparticles, the surface morphologies of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt changed a lot after the addition of H2PtCl6. The spherical Au@N-CQDs coated with Pt nanoparticles after the H2PtCl6 was reduced in AA for 45 min, generating core-shell Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles with diameters of 26 ± 2 nm (analyzed on three different regions, Fig. S2). However, despite great effort were made in the repeated TEM measurements, it is still no structure units with reliable characteristics of N-CQDs can be recognized. In addition, high-resolution TEM images exhibit good crystallinity of Pt shell with clear lattice fringes (Fig. 3 B inset picture). The lattice spacing distance is 0.229 nm, which is corresponding to the (111) planes of the face-centered cubic (fcc) Pt. Furthermore, EDX data are used to investigate the compositions of the nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. S2, the EDX spectrums of three different regions are displays two main peaks (Au and Pt) are clearly observed (Cu signal is from the Cu substrate) and the Au/Pt atomic ratio is about 1.5:1, confirming the formation of metal Au and Pt. Moreover, the FL emission peak becomes lower when H2PtCl6 was reduced on Au@N-CQDs (Fig. S1E).

The high angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) image of single Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles shown in Fig. 3 further shows the significant contrast between the core and the outer branches of nanoparticles. The elemental mapping also shows that the core of the nanoparticle is Au element, as Pt distributed at the external surface. From the results of mapping data, it reveals that Au and Pt are evenly distributed same as the morphology of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt core-shell nanoparticles. This is direct proof of the generating of an Au@N-CQDs@Pt core-shell structure. This structure of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles facilitate to their enhanced electrochemically active surface area and superior catalytic performance toward methanol oxidation. Additionally, we also prepared the nanoparticles with the aid of sodium citrate dehydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O) to explore the roles of N-CQDs played during synthesis process. Here, the Na3C6H5O7·2H2O is chosen as the contrastive reductant because it has been widely used in the preparation of Au nanoparticles. Fig. S3 gives the TEM image of the Au-Pt nanoparticles prepared by Na3C6H5O7·2H2O but N-CQDs. It can be found that the particles are also in spherical shape, but the absence of N-CQDs yield heavy aggregated nanoparticles. Therefore, it is demonstrate that the N-CQDs function as a reductant, stabilizer and a structure-directing agent play an essential role for the synthesis of the desired nanoparticles.

A comparative study of the functional groups on the N-CQDs, Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pt was performed using the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum. As shown in Fig. S4, the obvious peaks between 3000 and 3700 cm−1 can be attributed to the hydroxyl groups of N-CQDs [45]. Compared with the original N-CQDs, the relative intensity of hydroxyl groups (3000-3700 cm−1) decreased and the intensity of carbonyl groups (1715 cm−1) significantly increased after reaction with HAuCl4, indicating that hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs can reduce HAuCl4 to form Au@N-CQDs and convert into carbonyl groups after reaction. Therefore, the formation of Au nanoparticles is ascribed to the redox reaction between N-CQDs and AuCl4 − ions, which is similar to the reaction between HAuCl4 and graphene oxide [50]. In addition, the band at 1420 cm−1 is assigned to CN stretching vibrations [51]. The characteristic bands of the N-CQDs are also show in Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pt, indicating the successful modification of Au nanoparticles surface with N-CQDs.

XRD was measured to characterize the crystal structure of Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45. Fig. 4 provides XRD patterns of N-CQDs, Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45. In XRD pattern, the presence of a small broader heave with 2θ = 26° is assigned to N-doped carbon [32], both of Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45 exhibit an wide diffraction peak corresponding to graphite (002) planes, indicating the presence of N-CQDs. From XRD image of Au@N-CQDs composite, the (111), (200), (220) planes of metallic Au (JCPDS 04–0784) can be observed. Compared with Au@N-CQDs, the diffraction peak of Pt (111), Pt (200), Pt (220) is distinctly observed (JCPDS 04–0802), which conforme that the face-centered cubic (fcc) crystal structure of Pt. Based on the discussion of TEM and XRD, the Au@N-CQDs@Pt catalysts is indeed formed. Raman spectra of the different synthesized samples including N-CQDs, Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pt are detailedly investigated in Fig. S5. The Raman spectrum of N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs composite shows the D-band and G-band of carbon at 1343 and 1611 cm−1. It is well-known that the D-band corresponds to sp3 carbon atom vibrations and the G-band is associated with vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms [52]. Both N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs show a D-band and a G-band in Raman spectra, but the D-band and G-band disappear when Pt shell formed on Au@N-CQDs. Therefore, based on Raman results, we can propose that Au@N-CQDs@Pt have core-shell structures with Au@N-CQDs as the core and Pt as the shell.

XPS was measured to investigate the detail of composition and electronic properties. As can be observed in Fig. 5 A, two main peaks center at 70.81 eV and 74.05 eV are assigned to Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2 of Pt(0) species, whereas the other two peaks locate at 72.04 eV and 75.25 eV are attributed to Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2 of Pt(Ⅱ) [53]. In addition, the spectrum of Au peaks can be fitted only two peaks detected at 83.52 eV and 87.33 eV, which are assigned to Au f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 of Au(0) state [54]. These characterizations of XPS indicate metallic Pt and Au have been formed. The results of XPS are display in Table 1 and we can obtain the Pt(0)/Pt(Ⅱ) ratio is ∼60.81/39.19 for Au@N-CQDs@Pt catalysts, indicating that Au@N-CQDs@Pt electrocatalysts have good catalytic performance and stability owing to the special properties of Pt(0) components [55]. The C1s spectrum of Au@N-CQDs@Pt can be well fitted three peaks located at 284.3, 286.4, and 288.5 eV (Fig. S6A), which can be ascribed to CC, CC, and OCOH [56]. From the N1s spectrum in Fig. S6B, the three deconvoluted peaks at 399.5, 399.8 and 400.1 eV are assigned to NC, CN/NH and CN/NH groups, which confirms that the nitrogen elements are exist [57]. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) of Au@N-CQDs@Pt are shown in Fig. S7. It can be concluded that N-CQDs account for about 22 wt % of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt.

To further understand the growth mechanism and relationship between the catalytic activity and external feature of the composite, a series of different AA reaction time experiments were performed during synthesis process. When the reaction time was 10 min (Fig. 6 A and B), the Pt nanoparticles appeared. However, only a few of Au cores were covered with Pt nanodendrites. As reaction times increased to 45 min (Fig. 6C and D), more Pt nanodendrites were coated on the Au@N-CQDs surface, resulting in an increased Pt shell thickness. At a reaction time of 90 min, the nanoparticles became denser and less uniformly (Fig. 6E and F). After a period of 90 min, the product morphology was not changed. Additionally, it is worth mentioning that the morphology of nanoparticles nearly consistent at the entire reaction times and the average size of the Pt nanoparticles maintain around 3 nm over the process. These TEM results also confirm that the amount of Pt nanoparticles on the Au@N-CQDs surface can be controlled through changing the Pt deposit time.

The reaction process of the Pt nanoparticles growth on the Au@N-CQDs was examined by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Fig. 7 displays the spectra of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles from different solutions with various reaction times. The Au@N-CQDs clearly shows a characteristic absorption peak locate at 520 nm, which assigned to the surface plasmon resonance of Au. For Au@N-CQDs@Pt with reaction time of 30 min, red shift and flat of the absorption peak could be easily noticed which may be caused by overgrowth of the Pt nanoparticles shell. For longer reaction time, the absorption peaks gradually disappear and the spectroscopy remains basically unchanged, as a result of increasing Pt shell thickness.

During the preparation of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt core-shell nanoparticles, we also could estimate the process of the reaction through the transformation of the mixture color. As observed in Fig. 8 , the color of the initial Au@N-CQDs solution was light red. After adding H2PtCl6 solution and ascorbic acid (AA) solution, the color gradually changed to dark purple, because of deposition of the Pt nanoparticles on the Au@N-CQDs. Finally the solution turned dark black, indicating that the core-shell structure nanoparticles were totally formed.

In order to investigate the interfacial process and kinetics of electrode reactions in standard three-electrode system, other electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) has been employed to evaluate in the Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4− solution. It is worth noting that the value of electron transfer resistance (Ret) equals the diameter of the primary semicircle which represents the charge-transfer kinetics of the obtained electrocatalysts. A smaller diameter of the impedance arc (DIA) frequently represents a smaller charge-transfer resistance in Fe(CN)6 3−/4- system. From Fig. S8, it can be observed that the value order of charge transfer resistance from different electrodes is Rct(Au@N-CQDs@Pt) < Rct(Au@N-CQDs) < Rct(GCE)< Rct(N-CQDs), the oxygen groups on N-CQDs may result in a higher Rct for N-CQDs [58]. EIS provide additional evidence that Au@N-CQDs@Pt obtained in this work has the excellent conductivity.

Thanks to their attractive structure, the prepared Au@N-CQDs@Pt core-shell nanoparticles are expected to have a big electrochemically active surface area and considerable high electrochemical performance. Hence, the methanol oxidation experiments were conducted to estimate the catalytic activities of the prepared Au@N-CQDs@Pt nanoparticles because of its potential application in DMFCs. The catalysts with different reaction time were compared toward methanol electrooxidation to search a suitable reaction time. Fig. 9 shows the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, CVs and chronoamperometric (CA) curves of the different catalysts in 1 M NaOH solution comprising 0.5 M methanol.

The forward peak current density (Jf) and potential (Ef), the backward peak current density (Jb) and potential (Eb), and the current density (JCA) after polarizing 3000 s at the Ef from Fig. 9 are display in Table 2 . Overall, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45 exhibited a best catalytic activity compared with other Au@N-CQDs@Pt catalysts. As longer reaction times, the electrocatalysts exhibit a decreased catalytic activity. In an electrocatalytic reaction, one of major factors for methanol oxidation efficiency is the ability of methanol to access a catalyst surface [59]. At longer reaction times, a lot of Pt nanoparticles became aggregated, covering catalytic active sites on Pt and restraining reactants getting the inner region interface of Pt and Au@N-CQDs. This confined transport likely lead to a decreased catalytic activity at longer reaction times. Based upon above evaluations, Au@N-CQDs@Pt-45 was chose as the representative of Au@N-CQDs@Pt catalyst to compare with commercial Pt/C.

Fig. 10 A shows CV curves of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt, Au-Pt, Pt/N-CQDs and Pt/C catalysts tested in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. There is a clear peak in the forward step about −0.1 V is ascribed to desorption of adsorbed hydrogen on the Pt surface. This is followed by the Pt surface oxidation, which takes place for potential value higher than 0.7 V vs SCE. After the reduction of this Pt oxide at about 0.5 V, the adsorption of hydrogen starts at 0.2 V vs SCE in the reverse scan. Electrochemical active surface area (EASA) of these catalysts was estimated according to the following equation [60,61]:
EASA =
Q
H
/
( m ∗ 0.21 )
where QH (mC cm−2) represent the charge transfers due to hydrogen desorption, m (mg cm−2) represents the Pt loading mass on the GCE surface, and 0.21 is a constants means the charge required for oxide a monolayer adsorption of hydrogen on Pt surface. The calculated EASA of Au@N-CQDs@Pt, Au-Pt nanoparticles, Pt/N-CQDs and commercial Pt/C are 11.78, 11.03, 16.55, 8.18 m2 g−1 Pt, respectively. Compared to other two Au-Pt nanoparticles and Pt/C, the as-prepared Au@N-CQDs@Pt exhibited the larger EASA.

Electrooxidation of methanol on different type of catalysts were tested in a mixture containing 1 M NaOH and 0.5 M methanol at ambient temperature (Fig. 10B). The forward oxidation peaks observed at about −0.2 V is associated with the oxidation of freshly chemisorbed methanol, which can directly reflect the catalytic activity of the catalysts [62], accompanied with the appearance of another anodic peak at −0.3 V in the reverse scan corresponding to the oxidation of adsorbed carbonaceous component such as HCOO− and CO produced during the forward scan [63]. The values corresponding to peak current density are summarized in Table 3 . Based on the table, it can be seen that the Au@N-CQDs@Pt shows the outstanding mass activity of 587.11 mA mg−1 Pt, much higher than those Au-Pt (390.78 mA mg−1 Pt), Pt/N-CQDs (308.37 mA mg−1 Pt) and Pt/C (156.70 mA mg−1 Pt). The MOR current density at the Au@N-CQDs@Pt hybrids is 3.7 times bigger than that at commercial Pt/C. What's more, the Au@N-CQDs@Pt has shown higher If/Ib ratio than Au-Pt nanoparticles and is close to commercial Pt/C.

For the evaluation the long-term stability of Au@N-CQDs@Pt, chronoamperometry curves were recorded for a period of 3000 s. Fig. 10C show the i-t curves, from which it can be seen that Au@N-CQDs@Pt exhibits the slowest current decay during the entire process and have the highest final current density. After 3000 s sustaining measurement, the final current density of the Au@N-CQDs@Pt, Au-Pt nanoparticles, Pt/N-CQDs and commercial Pt/C are 21.52, 7.49, 10.29, 5.11 mA mg−1 Pt, respectively. The reason for better catalytic performance of Au@N-CQDs@Pt mainly originates from the follow reasons: small diameter N-CQDs and the oxygen groups on their surface could offer a lot of binding sites [64], which contributes to the formation of small grain size Pt nanoparticles and prevent the stack of Pt particles; In addition, the functional groups on the N-CQDs surface will improve the anti-poisonous abilities of Pt nanoparticles; The interfacial interaction between the Pt nanoparticles shell and Au@N-CQDs core may change their electronic and geometric structures.

In this study, a novel strategy for the preparation of Au@N-CQDs@Pt triple-layered structure nanoparticles with an Au metal core, N-CQDs interlayer, and Pt outer shell is reported. N-CQDs play a vital role in constructing core-shell structure through conducting contrast experiment. More importantly, benefiting from the core-shell structure and N-CQDs, the as-prepared nanocomposites have exceptionally improved electrocatalytic performance for MOR compared with Au-Pt and commercial Pt/C, exhibiting their potential application in DMFC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21165016, 21175108, 21265018) and the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (Nos. 1011GKCA025, 090GKCA036, 1208RJZM289) and the Innovation Capacity Improvement Plan of Northwest Normal University (CX2018Y007).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2019.04.212.
